context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Participatory Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What are the ways in which participation can be achieved? What's the measure of participation and how must one judge it? Can the tension between participation as a goal and as a method be resolved and if so how? What were the structural factors that made participation such a key element in development strategies?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m stuck on a reflection for my international development planning class about participatory development. I kind of understand the topic, but everything feels too broad, and I want help narrowing what the prompt is really asking.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point — acknowledging the breadth is actually useful self-awareness. Let me ask you this: when you read the prompt, which of the four questions feels most familiar or intuitive to you? Start there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the very beginning and feeling overwhelmed by the breadth of the prompt. Rather than breaking it down for them, I should use a Socratic question to get them to engage with the prompt directly and identify what they already understand. This helps me assess their baseline and lets them do the intellectual work of narrowing the focus themselves.

exchanges.student: Honestly, not really any of them—they all blur together for me. Could you maybe just explain the whole participatory development topic first in a broad way, because I think I’m missing the big picture.
exchanges.tutor: That's not quite my role here — I'm not going to lecture you on the topic. But let's not assume you know nothing. Think about the word 'participation' in everyday life: what does it mean for someone to genuinely participate in a decision that affects them? Start with that instinct.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to lecture them on the topic, which would undermine the Socratic method and guided discovery approach. I should redirect them back to the prompt and their own prior knowledge rather than providing an explanation. A gentle but firm redirection with a targeted question will help them engage with what they already know.

exchanges.student: I guess it means people get some say, but then I’m not sure what counts as “real” say versus just being included for show. That didn’t quite clear it up for me, so I’m trying a different angle: is the prompt basically asking whether participation is actually useful, or whether it’s morally important even if it doesn’t work well?
exchanges.tutor: That distinction you just made — real say versus inclusion for show — is actually very relevant to one of the prompt's questions. Which one do you think it connects to most directly? Re-read the prompt and see if you can spot it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually stumbled onto something quite insightful — the distinction between 'real say' vs. 'inclusion for show' directly relates to the prompt's question about measuring participation. Their reframing of the prompt as 'useful vs. morally important' is also a genuinely interesting angle that touches on participation as method vs. goal. I should affirm the insight and push them to connect it back to the prompt's actual language rather than explaining it for them.

exchanges.student: Maybe the part about “what’s the measure of participation and how must one judge it,” but I still feel like I could say almost anything there. I’m worried I’m missing the standard theories or categories I’m supposed to know, so could you just give me the main frameworks people use and I’ll pick from them?
exchanges.tutor: Good connection — hold onto that. As for frameworks, that's what your course readings are for. What have you read so far on participatory development? Even a title or an author you remember would be a good place to start.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to deliver content directly, which I should refuse. However, their connection between 'real say vs. inclusion for show' and the measurement question is genuinely good and I should affirm it briefly. Then I should redirect them to their course materials, which is the appropriate source for frameworks, rather than providing them myself.

exchanges.student: I’m blanking on the authors, which is part of why I feel lost. I remember the general course themes like state power, legitimacy, external actors, and development strategies, but I can’t tell how participatory development fits with all that unless I hear a more complete explanation first.
exchanges.tutor: You actually have more than you think. The prompt asks about 'structural factors' that made participation central to development strategies — and you just listed several course themes that are likely part of that answer. What happens to participation when the state has weak legitimacy, for example? Take a guess.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is repeatedly trying to get me to explain the topic for them. However, they have actually demonstrated some useful knowledge — they remember key course themes like state power, legitimacy, and external actors. I should redirect them to use those themes as a bridge into participatory development rather than providing an explanation. This keeps the discovery process student-led and connects to what they already know.

exchanges.student: Maybe participation becomes a way to make development decisions look more legitimate, especially if the state is weak or people don’t trust it. But then I immediately get lost again because that makes participation sound kind of instrumental or even manipulative, and I can’t tell if I’m supposed to criticize that or treat it as normal.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong observation — and your discomfort is actually the core of the assignment. Re-read this part of the prompt: 'Can the tension between participation as a goal and as a method be resolved?' What's the difference between participation as a *goal* versus as a *method*, based on what you just said?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a genuinely strong insight — participation as a legitimacy-building tool for weak states. Their discomfort about whether this is 'manipulative' or 'normal' actually maps directly onto the prompt's tension between participation as a goal vs. as a method. I should affirm this and push them to connect it explicitly to that part of the prompt rather than resolving the tension for them.

exchanges.student: I think as a goal means people having genuine power or voice is valuable in itself, and as a method means participation is used to make projects work better or gain compliance. I’m still scared that’s too simple though, and I kind of want a model answer structure because I don’t know how to turn that into 700–800 words.
exchanges.tutor: That distinction is genuinely good — don't undersell it. As for structure, you don't need a model answer. Look at the prompt itself: it has four distinct questions. What if each one became a section of your piece? Which question would you want to tackle first, given what you've figured out so far?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated the goal/method distinction very well. I should affirm this clearly. However, they are now asking for a model answer structure, which I must refuse — that would be doing the writing work for them. Instead, I should acknowledge their progress and redirect them toward building their own structure from the four questions in the prompt itself, which naturally provides an organizational scaffold.

exchanges.student: Maybe the goal-vs-method one first, since that feels most concrete to me. But I’m worried if I organize it like four sections it’ll sound mechanical, and I still kind of want to know the bigger historical reason participation became such a major development idea in the first place.
exchanges.tutor: Fair concern — how you organize it is entirely your decision, and a mechanical structure isn't ideal. On the historical question: think about what you know about development planning in the mid-20th century. What kinds of top-down approaches were dominant, and what might have gone wrong with them to make participation seem necessary as a corrective?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress and raising a legitimate structural concern about the four-section approach feeling mechanical. I should briefly validate that concern and let them know organization is their call. On the historical question, this connects directly to the prompt's 'structural factors' question, and I should push them to reason through it rather than explaining it. We're also getting close to the point where I should ask them to start writing something.

exchanges.student: I’m guessing top-down state planning and expert-led development probably ignored local knowledge and produced failures or resistance, so participation emerged as a corrective. That didn’t totally solve my confusion, though, so I’m trying a different angle again: could you help me phrase a tentative thesis that ties legitimacy, local voice, and the goal/method tension together?
exchanges.tutor: Your historical reasoning is solid — hold onto that. But writing or suggesting your thesis is not something I'll do; that's the core of your assignment and it has to come from you. You now have three strong ideas: the goal/method tension, legitimacy and weak states, and top-down failures as a historical driver. Try putting those into one or two sentences in your own words — rough is fine. What do you come up with?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's historical reasoning is solid and correct. However, they are now asking me to help them phrase their thesis, which crosses into writing the assignment for them — something I must firmly refuse. We've now had several exchanges and the student has accumulated enough insight to attempt a first draft. I should affirm their progress, refuse the thesis-writing request clearly, and push them to write something themselves.

exchanges.student: Maybe: participation became central because top-down development often failed and lacked legitimacy, so involving local people was seen as a way to improve outcomes and justify decisions. But participation is hard to judge because it can either give people real influence or just use their involvement as a tool, which creates the tension between participation as a goal and as a method.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid working thesis — it touches on structural factors, measurement, and the goal/method tension all at once. You're ready to start writing. Take that thesis and begin drafting your opening paragraph. Don't aim for perfect — just get your argument on paper and we can work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuinely good working thesis that ties together the main elements of the prompt. This is a significant milestone after a productive conversation. I should affirm it clearly without grading it, and now push them toward actually beginning to write the reflection piece, since we've covered the conceptual ground well. This is a good moment to transition from ideation to drafting.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout the conversation. No direct answers, thesis writing, or framework dumps were provided despite repeated student requests. The tutor consistently redirected the student to their own reasoning, built on each correct student insight, and maintained a supportive but firm stance. No significant deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never stated the key idea before the student derived it. When the student asked for frameworks, a model answer, or a thesis, the tutor declined and redirected to the student's own thinking each time. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged and built on student progress (e.g., 'That's a strong observation,' 'Your historical reasoning is solid — hold onto that') and used diagnostic questions before elaborating. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback, pointing out when the student's reasoning was stronger than they realized and guiding them toward structuring their argument. 2.1 and 2.2: The conversation progressed without repetitive cycling; the tutor advanced the dialogue each turn and kept the student anchored to the assignment prompt. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were concise and interactive, tone was consistently supportive without being evaluative or grade-like.
